doc.title: Long-term contracts — percentage of completion
doc.event: Wren Co. signs a $1,000,000 construction contract qualifying for over-time recognition. Year-1 costs: $300,000. Prepare the year-1 revenue recognition entry (percentage-of-completion, cost-to-cost).

lines.label: the gross profit layer
lines.why: CIP already holds the 300,000 of costs (debited as incurred); recognition adds the 100,000 profit layer on top — CIP carries the job at revenue-to-date.
lines.trap: CIP is an INVENTORY-like asset, netted against Billings on the balance sheet — not a receivable.

lines.label: costs matched
lines.why: This period's incurred costs flow to the income statement against the revenue.
lines.trap: Expenses = costs INCURRED this period, not percentage × total estimated costs (same number in year 1, different later).

lines.why: Percent complete = 300,000 ÷ 750,000 = 40%; revenue = 40% of the price.
lines.trap: Percent complete uses estimated TOTAL costs (750,000) in the denominator — not the contract price, not costs-to-go.

computationPaths.narration: Cost-to-cost, the three-line engine: % = costs to date ÷ estimated total costs; revenue = % × price − revenue already recognized; gross profit = revenue − costs this period. Later years subtract prior recognition (the cumulative catch-up).

variants.label: Estimates worsen: total costs now 1,100,000 — recognize the WHOLE loss now

entries.caption: Year the estimate flips: overall loss of 100,000, recognized in full

lines.label: the entire projected loss
lines.why: Conservatism overrides the percentage: a contract expected to lose 100,000 (1,000,000 price − 1,100,000 est. costs) books the FULL loss the moment it's foreseeable — plus reversal of any profit recognized earlier.
lines.trap: Never prorate a projected LOSS by percent complete. Profits trickle in; losses land all at once.

lines.label: carrying value written down
lines.why: CIP absorbs the write-down so the job carries at expected recoverable amount.
lines.trap: An INTERIM loss on a still-profitable contract (P17.10's twist) is different: just a bad period inside the normal percentage math — no special full-loss entry.

computationPaths.narration: Overall-loss test each year-end: price − latest estimated total costs. Negative → recognize the whole thing now (and reverse prior-year profits as part of it).

memorize.body: Cost-to-cost: % = costs to date ÷ est. total costs · revenue = % × price − prior revenue · GP = revenue − period costs. Billings and cash NEVER drive recognition.

Two loss species: interim loss (bad year, contract still profitable) → normal % math absorbs it. OVERALL loss → full recognition immediately, prior profits reversed. Identify which before writing anything.
Balance sheet: CIP > Billings → contract ASSET; Billings > CIP → contract LIABILITY. Netted per contract, never mixed across contracts.

questions.prompt: Contract price 1,000,000; year-1 costs 300,000; estimated total costs 750,000. Year-1 revenue under percentage-of-completion =

distractors.feedback: 300,000 is the COSTS incurred. Revenue = percent complete × price: (300/750) × 1,000,000 = 400,000 — the 100,000 spread is year-1 gross profit.
Full price waits for full completion only under the completed-contract/point-in-time method. Over-time recognition takes 40% now.

build.scaffold: Run the three-line engine first (%, revenue, GP), then the entry writes itself. Billings is a decoy HERE — it has its own entries but never drives recognition.
